name: Aisha bint Abu Bakr
title: Aisha bint Abu Bakr (RA): The Love Story
meta_description: The story of Aisha bint Abu Bakr (RA), the wife the Prophet Muhammad named as the most beloved person to him, and the home she remembered for the rest of her life.
keywords: Aisha bint Abu Bakr, story of Aisha, wife of Prophet Muhammad, mothers of the believers, love of the Prophet, sahaba stories
lede: When a companion asked the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, in front of everyone, who was the most beloved person in the world to him, the man expected to hear his own name. The answer came without hesitation: Aisha.
This is the story of Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her): the wife the Prophet ﷺ loved openly, the woman who would spend fifty years telling the world what he was like at home.

paras: Amr ibn al-As had come to Islam late, after years of opposing the Prophet ﷺ. The kindness he was shown after he believed was so complete that he began to wonder if he himself might be the most beloved person to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. So one day, in front of everyone, he asked.

The Prophet ﷺ answered at once: Aisha. Amr clarified that he meant the men, not the women. The Prophet ﷺ said, "Then her father," meaning Abu Bakr. Then Umar, then others. Amr said he stopped asking, certain his own name would never come.

It taught the companions two things. The Prophet ﷺ could treat a former enemy with such warmth that the man imagined himself the most loved of all. And the Prophet ﷺ felt no embarrassment in saying, plainly, that the most beloved person in the world to him was his wife.

sections.h2: A heart he could not command
paras: The whole community knew his love for Aisha. The Ansar noticed it too, and began saving their gifts for the day the Prophet ﷺ was with her, hoping to please him. The other wives felt the imbalance, and asked that gifts be welcomed in every home equally.

They sent messengers to raise it: first Umm Salama, then Fatima, then Zaynab. To Fatima, his own daughter, he simply asked, "My daughter, do you not love what I love?" To the wives he explained that revelation often came to him while he was with Aisha, and that this was not something he could control.

What he did privately is one of the most moving lines Aisha narrates. He divided his time and care among his family with perfect fairness, wronging no one. But he knew his heart leaned. So he would pray: "O Allah, this is my division in what I control. Do not blame me for what You control and I do not." He meant his own heart.

sections.h2: What he was like at home
paras: Through Aisha we have more than two thousand reports of the Prophet ﷺ inside his own house, and they read like nothing the world expected of a man of his standing. He came home smiling. Unless the matter was serious, he found a way to make it light. He mended his own clothes, patched his own shoes, and served the people of his household.

He was attentive in small, deliberate ways. He would hold her hand while he spoke to her, and turn his whole body to face her, the same full attention he gave to everyone. He drank from the cup where she had placed her lips, and ate from the same spot on the bone she had eaten from.

Aisha recalled these things with a smile, sometimes with laughter, sometimes with tears. Even in the last ten nights of Ramadan, when he withdrew to the mosque in devotion, he would lean his head into her room so she could comb and oil his hair. He wanted her to know he was still thinking of her, even then.

sections.h2: I am to you like Abu Zar
paras: One day Aisha told him about eleven women who had once sat together describing their husbands. She remembered every word each woman said. The Prophet ﷺ did not rush her to prayer or to the next expedition. He listened to the entire story.

The last woman, Umm Zar, praised a husband named Abu Zar who had treated her like a queen, before another marriage that never matched it. When Aisha finished, the Prophet ﷺ said, "I am to you like Abu Zar was to Umm Zar, except I would never divorce you."

She would sometimes ask him directly, "How is your love for me?" He would answer, "Like the knotted rope," meaning firm and unbroken. After an argument she might ask again how the knot was holding, and he would smile and tell her it was exactly as it had always been.

sections.h2: Tested by jealousy, met with calm
paras: Aisha was, by her own account, jealous over the Prophet ﷺ in a way she was jealous of no one else. Once, while companions sat in her home, another wife sent over a dish of food. Aisha came out, took the dish, and broke it in front of them all. The room fell silent, waiting.

He did not rebuke her. He said gently, "Your mother got jealous," then told them to bring a fresh dish in its place and leave the broken one. He understood that her reaction came from the depth of her love, and he let it pass.

Her jealousy reached even to the past, to Khadijah, whom she had never met. When she once spoke slightingly of "that old woman," his face changed and reddened. He did not curse or throw anything. He simply said no, Allah had never given him anyone better, and began to list Khadijah's every kindness. Aisha learned where the line was, and never crossed it again.

sections.h2: Should I not be a grateful servant
paras: When people asked Aisha for the most amazing thing she ever saw from him, she fell quiet and wept for a long time, until those around her wept too. Then she said his whole life was a wonder, but she would tell them of one night.

He had asked her leave to spend the night in worship. She answered, "I love being close to you, but I love even more what makes you happy." So he stood and prayed, and wept until his beard was wet, until the ground before him was wet. When she asked why he wept, though Allah had forgiven him, he said, "Should I not be a grateful servant?"

That night Allah had revealed to him an ayah, and he said: woe to the one who reads it and does not reflect on it. It was the verse about the signs woven into creation itself, the heavens, the earth, the turning of night and day, for those willing to think.

ayah.text: In the creation of the heavens and earth; in the alternation of night and day; in the ships that sail the seas with goods for people; in the water which God sends down from the sky to give life to the earth when it has been barren, scattering all kinds of creatures over it; in the changing of the winds and clouds that run their appointed courses between the sky and earth: there are signs in all these for those who use their minds.

paras: Aisha said that the thing she loved most about him was not his smile or his tenderness, but his worship. She watched his feet swell from standing in prayer and asked why he prayed so long when his sins were forgiven. He gave her the same answer: should he not be a grateful servant.

It was Aisha who carried so much of what we know of his nights, his fasting, and his quiet acts of devotion. He once told her that his eyes might sleep, but his heart never slept. A home that the world saw only as poor was, in truth, filled with revelation.

Their love was real, playful, and ordinary in its days, and yet it became a window onto the Prophet ﷺ that no public gathering could have given. She loved his nearness, and she loved his Lord even more, and in that order she found the most beautiful answer she could give him.

lessons_intro: Her story is not a single great event. It is the texture of an ordinary marriage lived honestly, and that is exactly why it still teaches.

lessons.bold: Love is shown in small attention.
lessons.text: He held her hand, faced her fully, raced her, and listened to her stories to the end. Tenderness lives in how we treat the people closest to us, not in grand gestures.

lessons.bold: Be fair in what you control.
lessons.text: He divided his care with perfect justice and asked Allah only about the leaning of his heart. We are answerable for our actions, not for every feeling, and that distinction brings peace.
When she broke the dish, he did not shame her. He named her jealousy gently and moved on. A soft answer in a sharp moment protects more than it ever costs.

lessons.bold: Love what pleases the One you love.
lessons.text: Given the choice, she chose his happiness over her own nearness. The love that lasts is the love that wants the good of the other above itself.

faq.q: Who was Aisha bint Abu Bakr?
faq.a: A wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the daughter of his closest companion, Abu Bakr. She is one of the Mothers of the Believers and one of the foremost narrators and teachers of his life and example.

faq.q: Why is Aisha called the most beloved?
faq.a: When asked in public who was the most beloved person to him, the Prophet ﷺ answered without hesitation: Aisha, and then her father Abu Bakr. He said it openly, without any embarrassment.

faq.q: What do we learn from Aisha about the Prophet's home life?
faq.a: Through more than two thousand of her narrations, we learn that he came home cheerful, mended his own clothes, served his household, was attentive and affectionate, and turned his nights to long, weeping prayer.

faq.q: What is the main lesson from the life of Aisha?
faq.a: That love is shown in steady attention and fairness, that calm answers defuse sharp moments, and that the deepest love wants the happiness of the other more than its own comfort.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is tempting to think that a great life is built from great moments. Aisha's memories say otherwise. What she carried for fifty years were the ordinary things: a hand held while he spoke, a cup shared, a story heard to the end, a fair division of an honest man's days.

Most of our own faith is lived the same way, at home, with the people who depend on us. Her story is an invitation to give that attention freely, to answer anger softly, and to love most of all the things that please Allah. Carry one of those into today, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Aisha (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), focusing on her marriage to the Prophet ﷺ. The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 2:164). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

social.caption:
When the Prophet ﷺ was asked, in front of everyone, who was the most beloved person in the world to him, he answered without hesitation: Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her).

For fifty years after he passed, she told the world what he was like at home: how he came in smiling, mended his own clothes, held her hand when he spoke, and turned his nights to long prayer. Through her, more than two thousand narrations let us see him.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The most beloved person to the Prophet ﷺ
email.preview: A companion asked, in front of everyone, who he loved most. The answer came at once.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

One day a companion asked the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, in front of everyone, who was the most beloved person in the world to him. The man expected to hear his own name. The answer came without hesitation: Aisha.

That was Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), the daughter of his closest friend, and one of the Mothers of the Believers. The whole community knew how he loved her, and he felt no embarrassment in saying it plainly.

What moves you, reading her narrations, is how ordinary the love was. He came home smiling. He mended his own clothes and served his household. He held her hand when he spoke to her, and turned his whole body to face her. When she asked, "How is your love for me?" he answered, "Like the knotted rope," firm and unbroken.

He was fair. He divided his care among his family without wronging anyone, and then asked Allah only about the leaning of his heart: "O Allah, this is my division in what I control. Do not blame me for what You control and I do not."

And when she once broke a dish of food in front of his guests, he did not shame her. He said gently, "Your mother got jealous," and called for a fresh dish in its place.

There is one night Aisha could barely speak of without weeping. He had asked her leave to spend it in worship. She told him, "I love being close to you, but I love even more what makes you happy." So he stood and prayed, and wept until the ground before him was wet. When she asked why, though he was forgiven, he said, "Should I not be a grateful servant?"

That is the lesson in her that is easy to miss: the love that lasts is the love that wants the other's good above its own. She loved his nearness, and she loved his Lord even more.

May Allah be pleased with her, and teach us to love what pleases Him.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: A companion once asked the Prophet ﷺ, in front of everyone, who was the most beloved person in the world to him. The man was sure his own name would come. Instead the Prophet ﷺ answered at once: Aisha. And then, he said, her father.

Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her) was the daughter of his closest friend. The whole community knew his love for her, and he felt no shame in saying it. Through her we have more than two thousand glimpses into his life at home.

He came in smiling. He mended his own clothes and patched his own shoes and served the people of his house. He held her hand when he spoke to her, and faced her fully, the same attention he gave to everyone. When she asked how strong his love was, he said it was like a knotted rope, firm and unbroken.

He was careful to be fair. He divided his time and care among his family wronging no one, yet he knew his heart leaned toward her. So he prayed quietly, "O Allah, this is my division in what I control. Do not blame me for what You control and I do not."

Her love showed itself in jealousy too. Once she broke a dish of food in front of his guests, and the room fell silent. He did not rebuke her. He said gently, "Your mother got jealous," and called for another dish in its place.

When people asked Aisha for the most amazing thing she ever saw from him, she wept before she could speak. Then she told them of a night he asked her leave to pray. "I love being close to you," she said, "but I love even more what makes you happy."

He stood and prayed, and wept until his beard was wet, until the ground before him was wet. She asked why he wept, though he was forgiven. He said, "Should I not be a grateful servant?" That night, he told her, an ayah had been revealed: woe to the one who reads it and does not reflect on it.
storybook.pullquote: I love being close to you, but I love even more what makes you happy.
storybook.reflection: Aisha's story is not one great moment but the texture of an ordinary marriage remembered with love. What she carried for fifty years were the small things: a hand held, a cup shared, a story heard to the end. And when she had to choose, she chose his happiness over her own nearness. The love that lasts is the love that wants the good of the other more than itself.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
